header.title: Covert medication — policy, best interest, documentation

body.summary: Giving medication without a person's knowledge is only ever legal in very specific circumstances — and every step must be documented and agreed in advance.
body.text:
TOPIC: Covert medication
DURATION TARGET: 4–5 minutes
ELEVENLABS VOICE SUGGESTION: Speaker A — "Aria" (warm, slightly anxious female), Speaker B — "Marcus" (calm, reassuring male)

[SPEAKER A]: Can I run something past you? I've been worried about it all morning. On my round, another carer told me to just stir Mr Okafor's tablets into his porridge. She said he always spits them out, so it's the only way he takes them. I did it, and now it doesn't sit right with me.

[SPEAKER B]: First of all, I'm glad you came to me. And I want to say this clearly: you were right to feel uneasy. Hiding medication in food or drink without the person knowing is called covert medication. It's only ever allowed in very specific circumstances, and on its own, a quick fix in the porridge isn't one of them.

[SPEAKER A]: So I've done something wrong?

[SPEAKER B]: You followed what a colleague told you, and now you're checking it. That's exactly what good practice looks like. The thing to understand is that this is never a decision one carer makes on the spot. It can only happen when the person has been properly assessed as not having the mental capacity to make this decision for themselves, and a formal plan is already in place.

[SPEAKER A]: But he does refuse. Every single time. Isn't it cruel to just let him miss his tablets?

[SPEAKER B]: That's a really caring question, and you're right to worry about him missing essential medicine. But here's the key part. If someone has the capacity to decide, they're allowed to refuse, even if we think it's unwise, and we have to respect that. Covert medication is only for when a capacity assessment shows they can't understand or weigh up the decision. "He always refuses" isn't enough on its own. Without that assessment and a plan, putting it in his porridge could actually count as abuse.

[SPEAKER A]: Abuse? That word scares me. I never meant any harm.

[SPEAKER B]: I know you didn't, and nobody's saying you're a bad carer. That's exactly why we have a proper process, so caring people aren't left guessing. So let me walk you through what should happen. First, his GP or prescriber has to agree the medicine is essential and authorise giving it covertly. Then a pharmacist checks each medicine separately to confirm it can be safely crushed or mixed, and tells us which foods are safe and which to avoid.

[SPEAKER A]: I didn't know the pharmacist had to be involved at all.

[SPEAKER B]: They're vital. Some tablets stop working or even become harmful if you crush them, so we never guess. After that, there's a best-interest meeting. That brings together staff, the GP, the pharmacist, and someone who speaks for him, usually a family member or an advocate. Together they agree whether this is truly in his best interest.

[SPEAKER A]: Could his daughter just say yes on his behalf to save the meeting?

[SPEAKER B]: No, and that surprises a lot of people. No one, not even a close family member, can consent to medication for another adult. Her views matter and she's invited, but the decision is made as a team, in his best interest. Once it's all agreed, it goes into a written plan in his care records, and every covert dose gets recorded so there's a clear trail of what's given and when.

[SPEAKER A]: And then it just carries on?

[SPEAKER B]: No, it gets reviewed regularly, ideally every week, because it's never meant to be a long-term fix. If his condition changes, or he gets his capacity back and can consent, the plan has to be looked at again and may stop.

[SPEAKER A]: That makes me feel so much better. So for now, what do I actually do about this morning?

[SPEAKER B]: Tell me exactly what happened so I can record it, and we'll check whether Mr Okafor even has a plan in place. If he doesn't, we stop straight away and start the proper process with his GP. And from now on, if anyone ever asks you to hide medication, come to me first. Every time.

---
KEY TAKEAWAYS (for show notes / text card pairing):
1. Covert medication is only lawful when the person is assessed under the Mental Capacity Act as lacking capacity for this decision, and a refusal by someone with capacity must always be respected.
2. It requires a best-interest meeting involving the GP, the pharmacist, and family or an advocate, with a written plan and every dose recorded — never a one-off decision a single carer makes. No one, not even family, can consent for another adult.
3. The pharmacist must confirm each medicine can be safely disguised, and the plan must be reviewed regularly, ideally weekly, because covert medication is not a long-term solution.
